segments.title: The treaty category that small carriers never received
segments.summary: Fear of Japanese small-carrier construction discouraged a category that could have supported convoy protection and anti-submarine aviation.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the 1930 negotiations missed an opportunity to distinguish fleet carriers from smaller light or escort carriers. Larger ships were already understood to be more efficient for fleet aviation, yet fears that Japan might mass-produce small carriers shaped the rules. British experience with aviation in First World War anti-submarine operations and prewar studies of merchant conversions show that convoy carriers were not an idea invented only after war began.

evidence.note: The transcript connects treaty fears about small Japanese carriers with neglected convoy-carrier and merchant-conversion concepts.

segments.title: A two-tier fleet and light-carrier system
segments.summary: A proposed division of carrier tonnage between fleet and light ships explores how treaty rules could encourage a more varied aviation fleet.
segments.body: The video tests several ways to reserve most tonnage for fleet carriers while protecting a smaller allocation for light carriers. Exact arithmetic proves awkward, but the purpose is clear: prevent every navy from spending its entire allowance on one carrier type. For the Royal Navy, such a rule could have supported a repeating construction program of roughly 25,000-ton fleet carriers and 11,000- to 15,000-ton light carriers without a carrier-building holiday.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke works through fleet-carrier and light-carrier tonnage divisions and likely British construction choices.

segments.title: What Glorious and Courageous still offered
segments.summary: The converted battlecruisers remained useful 48-aircraft carriers whose survival could have eased pressure on Britain's wartime carrier force.
segments.body: Glorious and Courageous were imperfect conversions, but neither was militarily irrelevant. A fuller modernization could have extended their flight decks, hangars, lifts, and aircraft capacity. Had they survived, extra carriers at Mers-el-Kebir, during the Bismarck hunt, or at other crisis points could have added striking power while reducing wear on the rest of the fleet.

evidence.note: The transcript assesses modernization possibilities and wartime uses for Glorious and Courageous.

segments.title: Carrier building as Depression-era economic policy
segments.summary: The counterfactual keeps the Wall Street Crash but changes political choices so naval construction can support skilled industry and national recovery.
segments.body: The scenario does not remove the 1929 Wall Street Crash, because doing so would sever too many links with actual interwar history. Instead, governments adopt a more active Keynesian approach and treat naval orders as one form of economic intervention. Dr. Clarke substitutes Stanley Baldwin for Ramsay MacDonald and Al Smith for Herbert Hoover because he judges them more willing to fund defense and employment. That political change supplies the demand needed to keep carrier construction, design work, and skilled maritime labor moving through the Depression.

evidence.note: The transcript preserves the Wall Street Crash while using Keynesian policy, Baldwin, and Smith to justify greater naval construction.

segments.title: How naval treaties are negotiated outside the formal room
segments.summary: Formal speeches display agreed positions, while side conversations do much of the real work of assembling a treaty coalition.
segments.body: The conference chamber presents hierarchy and public positions, but much of the agreement is prepared in quieter discussions around it. Delegates normally test allied support before speaking formally and frame proposals to constrain or surprise opponents. This makes arms control a contest of coalition management and wording as much as ship numbers.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains the relationship between formal sessions, sidebars, allied support, and tactical wording.

segments.title: The 1930 treaty defines the aircraft carrier
segments.summary: Article 3 classifies purpose-built flight-deck ships as carriers while preventing existing capital ships from becoming hybrid battlecarriers.
segments.body: The treaty replaces the Washington definition with one centered on a warship's exclusive purpose and its ability to launch and land aircraft. Merely fitting a flying-off platform to another ship type did not make it a carrier. A further prohibition on landing decks for existing capital ships reflects anxiety that navies might combine battleship and carrier capabilities in one hull.

evidence.note: The transcript reads Article 3 and interprets its purpose-based carrier definition and capital-ship restriction.

segments.title: Why the 10,000-ton rule chilled small-carrier construction
segments.summary: Repeated restrictions around small carriers and guns created enough ambiguity to deter navies and their treasuries from using the category.
segments.body: Article 4 focuses on carriers of 10,000 tons or less carrying guns above 6.1 inches, then repeats restrictions on acquisition and construction. Dr. Clarke reads the repetition as more than harmless drafting: it made small-carrier projects appear politically risky even where a narrow legal reading might permit them. Article 5 then standardizes the carrier gun ceiling at 6.1 inches.

evidence.note: The transcript reads Articles 4 and 5 and explains how repetition and gun limits discouraged small-carrier programs.

segments.title: First World War trauma shaped the treaty mindset
segments.summary: British and French leaders negotiated from deep wartime loss, while Japan's different experience produced a sharply different view of risk and victory.
segments.body: King George V represents a wider governing class scarred by the destruction of friends, relatives, and a generation of junior leaders. That experience encouraged peace-seeking and caution in Britain and France. Japan had fought a comparatively successful and less costly war, so its leaders did not necessarily share the same assumptions; treaty makers risked mistaking their own trauma for a universal strategic outlook.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke links George V and elite wartime losses to peace policy, then contrasts Japanese experience and expectations.

segments.title: The 1936 treaty narrows carrier size and armament
segments.summary: The later treaty sets a 23,000-ton limit, caps heavy guns, defines flight-deck and non-flight-deck carrier categories, and permits replacement after twenty years.
segments.body: The 1936 rules lower maximum standard displacement to 23,000 tons and retain the 6.1-inch gun ceiling, with no more than ten guns above 5.25 inches. They also distinguish conventional flight-deck carriers from ships without such a deck, leaving seaplane carriers inside the framework. Ark Royal shows how much capability could still fit within roughly 22,000 long tons.

evidence.note: The transcript reads the 1936 displacement, gun, definition, category, and replacement provisions.

segments.title: A treaty needs ships Japan can actually build
segments.summary: A workable ratio could absorb Japanese resources, buy time, and reduce the incentive to abandon treaty limits for much larger carriers.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke returns to a 10:10:7:4:4 ratio because it would give Japan a visible gain while preserving Anglo-American superiority. A Japanese construction program would consume money, labor, and industrial capacity that could not simultaneously support every other military ambition. Permitting useful ships within the agreement could also make continued membership more attractive than a dramatic breakout. The proposal therefore treats construction as both a concession and a restraint: it buys time by giving Japan something valuable to lose.

evidence.note: The transcript links the proposed ratio with Japanese resource costs, treaty credibility, and the danger of an unrestricted breakout.

segments.title: British carrier numbers versus American carrier size
segments.summary: Washington Treaty allowances encouraged the Royal Navy to spread aviation across more hulls while the United States concentrated capability in much larger conversions.
segments.body: The Washington framework nominally allowed 27,000-ton carriers and larger 33,000-ton conversions. Courageous and Glorious displaced only about 24,210 long tons, even though Britain could have used more of the conversion allowance. Dr. Clarke connects that choice to the Royal Navy's preference for enough viable carriers to cover widely separated commitments. The contrast prepares the comparison with Lexington and Saratoga, where the United States concentrated far more tonnage and aviation capacity in each hull.

evidence.note: The transcript reviews Washington carrier limits, British conversion displacement, and the Royal Navy preference for numbers over maximum size.

segments.title: Why geography pushed Britain toward armoured carriers
segments.summary: British carrier design emphasized survival and repair because likely wars placed ships far from yards or within range of land-based aircraft.
segments.body: Illustrious carried fewer aircraft than Ark Royal because armour answered Britain's expected operating conditions. In the Far East, carriers would be remote from the home industrial base and needed damage that local facilities could repair. In the Mediterranean, Italian airfields made land-based attack unavoidable, so survivability mattered alongside air-group size.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Illustrious-style protection with Far Eastern repair limits and Mediterranean air threats.

segments.title: Lexington and Saratoga set the real carrier benchmark
segments.summary: The American conversions reached about 36,000 long tons, making them a more honest basis for equitable limits than smaller British conversions.
segments.body: Although often paired conceptually with Courageous and Glorious, Lexington and Saratoga were substantially larger ships. Their nominal 33,000-ton treaty figure gained another 3,000 tons through allowances for protection. Dr. Clarke argues that an equitable system should acknowledge that real benchmark and then divide a larger total allowance between fleet and light carriers.

evidence.note: The transcript compares British conversions with Lexington and Saratoga and explains the additional protection allowance.

segments.title: A steady carrier program instead of a wartime rush
segments.summary: Alternating fleet and light-carrier orders could preserve yards and deliver a balanced force gradually across a twenty-year replacement cycle.
segments.body: The counterfactual does not assume Britain instantly builds to its full treaty allowance. Instead, one fleet carrier and one light carrier enter production on alternating years, establishing a sustainable replacement rhythm. By 1939 this could yield several modern ships while keeping design teams, yards, and skilled labor active for emergency expansion.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke sketches alternating fleet- and light-carrier orders and plausible commissioning years.

segments.title: Ryūjō embodied the fear behind the small-carrier rules
segments.summary: Japan's attempt to combine fleet and escort-carrier value in a roughly 10,000-ton hull produced a useful but chronically top-heavy ship.
segments.body: The 1930 provisions brought sub-10,000-ton carriers into treaty tonnage and constrained their armament, making large programs less attractive. Ryūjō illustrates both why other powers worried and why the concept had limits. Japan tried to obtain substantial aviation utility from a very small displacement, but the resulting ship suffered serious topweight and operating problems.

evidence.note: The transcript summarizes the treaty effect and uses Ryūjō to show the utility and stability costs of a small carrier.

segments.title: The proposed equitable carrier settlement
segments.summary: A 10:10:7:4:4 ratio, a 31,000-ton new-build ceiling, and 310,000 total tons aim to give Japan security without surrendering Anglo-American superiority.
segments.body: The alternative starts from a ratio Japan might regard as a diplomatic victory while Britain and the United States retain meaningful superiority. Existing Lexington-class ships would be acknowledged, but future builds would be capped around 31,000 tons with 6.1-inch guns and a twenty-year replacement life. A ten-carrier, 310,000-ton ceiling offers flexibility in ship size without encouraging unlimited mass construction.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke states the proposed ratio, new-build limit, gun limit, replacement rule, ship count, and total tonnage.

segments.title: How more tonnage changes British and American carriers
segments.summary: Extra displacement could merge Britain's strike and fleet roles while giving American Yorktown successors larger air groups and better protection.
segments.body: Britain's historical division between an air-group-centered strike carrier and an armoured fleet carrier reflected tight design tradeoffs. Ships around 25,800 tons might combine Ark Royal's aviation capacity with more of Illustrious's protection. The United States could instead exploit a 31,000-ton ceiling for larger revised Yorktowns, avoiding the compromised Wasp and emphasizing Pacific air-group capacity.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts British strike and fleet roles and projects British and American designs under the larger allowance.

segments.title: Carrier construction strengthens industry and deterrence
segments.summary: A continuing building program creates both a stronger wartime production base and a larger force that adversaries must account for before choosing war.
segments.body: Peacetime construction does more than add hulls: it sustains the industrial capacity needed to rearm quickly. A visibly stronger British carrier force could also raise Germany's estimate of the force required for a successful war. Yet planners could not know the full effect of air power in advance, and militaries cannot safely stake national survival on untested theories alone.

evidence.note: The transcript links construction to industrial mobilization, deterrence, uncertainty about air power, and institutional caution.

segments.title: Higher treaty limits favor the strongest shipbuilding powers
segments.summary: Britain and America could exploit larger allowances faster than Japan because their maritime industries and economies already had greater depth.
segments.body: More tonnage need not mean only larger ships; navies could choose more 20,000- to 22,000-ton carriers instead. It could also buy protection, as later American interest in armoured carriers suggests. Because Britain and the United States possessed the largest maritime industrial bases, they were better positioned to reach a higher ceiling than Japan, which would have to sacrifice other construction to pursue its ratio.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke discusses alternative ship sizes, added protection, and the unequal industrial ability to use larger treaty allowances.

segments.title: Using limited fleets to bind Germany and the Soviet Union
segments.summary: Small treaty allocations might strengthen Weimar legitimacy while Soviet fleet construction divides attention between Germany and Japan.
segments.body: The proposed settlement could invite Germany and the Soviet Union into lower tiers rather than leave them outside the system. Soviet construction divided among the Baltic, Black Sea, Northern, and Pacific fleets would struggle to concentrate, but even a Far Eastern force would complicate Japanese planning. A legal naval program could also give the Weimar Republic a visible diplomatic success and maritime prestige that extremists might otherwise exploit. France would still retain twice Germany's allocation, while Britain would possess a much larger margin over Italy.

evidence.note: The transcript explores German and Soviet treaty tiers, Soviet fleet distribution, Japanese distraction, and Weimar political prestige.

segments.title: Why two carriers do not create a continuous capability
segments.summary: Carrier numbers must cover deployment, relief, training, refit, and dry-docking before a navy can guarantee that a ship is available.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses a simple readiness ladder to distinguish possession from operational capability. One carrier is largely a prestige asset, while two normally allow only one ship to be available at a time. Three improve the odds of coverage, but four are needed to support a deployed ship, an immediate relief, and vessels passing through refit, training, transit, or dry-docking. Germany's proposed two-carrier allowance would therefore create experience and options without guaranteeing continuous carrier operations.

evidence.note: The transcript explains how fleets of one through four carriers translate into deployment, relief, training, refit, and dry-docking availability.

segments.title: Multilateral limits instead of a later bilateral deal
segments.summary: Bringing Germany into the common treaty could impose limits without the diplomatic damage caused by the later Anglo-German Naval Agreement.
segments.body: A small German allocation inside a multilateral agreement would provide a limit that all the signatories could monitor. It might remove the perceived need for the later Anglo-German Naval Agreement, which created friction with France. Soviet participation could similarly create a legal fleet while diverting Japanese attention in the Far East. Dr. Clarke's wider conclusion is that an equitable settlement could pursue hard-nosed balance-of-power aims without repeating the humiliating, short-term victories that made later cooperation less likely.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts multilateral limits with the later Anglo-German Naval Agreement and warns against another crushing treaty victory.

segments.title: Treaty tonnage, not indifference, constrained British carriers
segments.summary: Britain already possessed the world's largest carrier count in 1930, but its 135,000-ton ceiling sharply limited replacement choices.
segments.body: The alternative allowance would not automatically produce every permitted ship, and older carriers might still need to be retired. The central point is that Britain had six carriers in 1930 and soon seven, demonstrating institutional commitment to naval aviation. Implacable's later displacement shows how an extra few thousand tons could materially improve a British fleet carrier without demanding a radically larger design.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes treaty permission from actual construction and compares Britain's carrier count, tonnage limit, and Implacable displacement.
